feed.title: Combined summary - Proposal discussion: BIP39 native-language display wordlists

entry.title: Combined summary - Proposal discussion: BIP39 native-language display wordlists
entry.summary: The proposal to enhance Bitcoin wallet recovery by introducing native language support for BIP39 recovery phrases focuses on improving user experience without altering the cryptographic integrity of the process. The initiative aims to allow users to view and input their recovery phrases in their native languages, while still relying on the canonical English BIP39 wordlist for cryptographic operations. This approach addresses the common challenges faced by non-English speakers, such as spelling errors and misunderstandings, which can compromise the recovery process. By mapping native language phrases to the corresponding indices of the English list, the solution ensures that the cryptographic process involving PBKDF2 remains unchanged, thus maintaining security. The draft implementation and initial wordlists are open for community feedback at [this GitHub repository](https://github.com/osem23/bip39-wordlists-tzur), seeking insights particularly into the appropriateness of this method for the BIP (Bitcoin Improvement Proposal) process. The feedback phase is crucial to identify potential risks, determine the practical value for wallet developers, and establish guidelines for native speaker review and normalization rules. Furthermore, any existing studies or prior work related to this concept could significantly inform the development of the proposal, ensuring a well-rounded and effective implementation. Daniel Osemberg's email highlights a fundamental issue with the current BIP39 standard, where using different languages for seed phrase generation leads to compatibility issues due to varying master keys derived from the seed phrases encoded in different languages. He stresses the need for clarity between "locale-mapped seed phrases" under the new specification and legacy seed phrases in their original language formats to prevent misderived keys and potential fund losses. The proposal does not aim to create a new seed phrase format but rather to define fixed, independent display wordlists that deterministically map to the canonical English BIP39 word indexes. This would help maintain the distinction between legacy and modern mnemonic systems, preventing confusion and enhancing the security and integrity of wallet recoveries across various languages. The proposed system maintains a structured sequence in handling TZUR display mnemonics, starting from localized display words to canonical English BIP39 words, followed by standard checksum validation and cryptographic derivation processes. This method preserves the security of the original English mnemonic while accommodating multilingual user interfaces. To ensure clarity and avoid ambiguity in wallet implementations, it is suggested that wallets explicitly identify the wordlist mode being used—whether it's a legacy non-English BIP39, a TZUR display wordlist, or the canonical English BIP39—with stable identifiers like language code and hash of the wordlist file. This metadata should be stored alongside the wallet data to facilitate accurate restoration and improve portability.
